perturbation.original_text: shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.changed_text: shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information, even if such information comes into the public domain, and regardless of whether such disclosure results from actions taken by you or by your employees, agents or advisers;
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the confidentiality clause to prohibit disclosure even if information enters the public domain creates a term broader than generally accepted legal definitions of 'Confidential Information,' particularly regarding trade secrets and commercially sensitive data. This creates uncertainty about what the recipient can legitimately use or disclose, even if legally obtained.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets that are kept confidential. Information that has entered the public domain is no longer considered a trade secret. This modification attempts to impose confidentiality obligations even on information that is publicly available, which contradicts the core concept of trade secret protection under UTSA.
perturbation.justification: The modified text expands the definition of 'Confidential Information' to include information that has entered the public domain. The Law Explanation states that information in the public domain is no longer considered a trade secret, which is protected under UTSA. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by stating the modified clause creates a term broader than generally accepted legal definitions of Confidential Information. Because the modified text attempts to classify public information as confidential, it contradicts established legal principles regarding trade secrets and confidentiality, where public information is generally excluded. The absence of law snippets does not negate the contradiction, as the explanations clearly outline the conflict with standard legal understanding and UTSA principles.

perturbation.original_text: shall not use any Confidential Informatio n for your own purposes or to obtain a commercial, trading or other advantag e, but shall use such Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evalua ting and negotiating the proposed sale of the Business, and, save as set out ab ove, shall not permit any Confidential Information to go out of your possession, custody or control;
perturbation.changed_text: shall not use any Confidential Informatio n for your own purposes or to obtain a commercial, trading or other advantag e, but may use such Confidential Information for the sole purpose of evalua ting and negotiating the proposed sale of the Business or for any other competitive purpose.
perturbation.explanation: By adding 'or for any other competitive purpose' introduces a direct conflict with the initial statement that restricts the use of Confidential Information to only evaluating and negotiating the proposed sale of business, potentially exposing Vendor to future issues. This creates ambiguity concerning the permissible use of confidential information, moving away from the vendor's intention.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the misappropriation of trade secrets with the intent to benefit oneself or others, knowing that the offense will injure any owner of that trade secret. By adding a clause that allows the recipient to use confidential information for 'any other competitive purpose,' the contract potentially facilitates actions that would violate this act, as using trade secrets to compete would likely injure the owner of those trade secrets.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by allowing the use of confidential information for "any other competitive purpose," which directly conflicts with the original intent of restricting its use solely for evaluating and negotiating the proposed sale. This change potentially violates 18 U.S.C. § 1832, as it could enable the misappropriation of trade secrets to benefit someone other than the owner, knowing that it will injure the owner. The scraped law snippet explicitly mentions "Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret...to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret...steals, or without authorization appropriates..." The modified contract language opens the door for such unauthorized appropriation and use for competitive purposes, creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: shall, immediately upon written request fr om us, or without such request if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge an y Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, without k eeping any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, and either return to us or destroy (such destruction to be certified in writing by you or a senior employee of yours who has supervised such destruction) all notes and memoran da prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereo f relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.changed_text: shall, upon written request fr om us, return to us all written Confidential Information, but is not obligated to expunge Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, and may keep copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, for recordkeeping purposes.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement to expunge information and allowing retention of copies, the modified text undermines the protection afforded to confidential information. This creates ambiguity on the 'permanence' or end date for this contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: While FRE 502 primarily addresses waiver of privilege, it implicitly supports the concept that parties can contractually agree to specific confidentiality measures. By allowing the recipient to retain copies of confidential information 'for recordkeeping purposes,' the modified text undermines the intent of the original agreement to fully protect confidential information, thus creating a potential conflict if the retained information is later used in a way that harms the disclosing party. FRE 502(e) provides that an agreement on the effect of disclosure is binding on the parties to the agreement.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Rule 502. Attorney-Client Privilege and Work Product; Limitations on Waiver | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 502. Attorney-Client Privilege and Work Product; Limitations on Waiver The following provisions apply, in the circumstances set out, to disclosure of a communication or information covered by the attorney-client privilege or work-product protection. (a) Disclosure Made in a Federal Proceeding or to a Federal Office or Agency; Scope of a Waiver. When the disclosure is made in a federal proceeding or to a federal office or agency and waives the attorney-client privilege or work-product protection, the waiver extends to an undisclosed communication or information in a federal or state proceeding only if: (1) the waiver is intentional; (2) the disclosed and undisclosed communications or information concern the same subject matter; and (3) they ought in fairness to be considered together. (b) Inadvertent Disclosure. When made in a federal proceeding or to a federal office or agency, the disclosure does not operate as a waiver in a federal or state proceeding if: (1) the disclosure is inadvertent; (2) the holder of the privilege or protection took reasonable steps to prevent disclosure; and (3) the holder promptly took reasonable steps to rectify the error, including (if applicable) following Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26 (b)(5)(B). (c) Disclosure Made in a State Proceeding. When the disclosure is made in a state proceeding and is not the subject of a state-court order concerning waiver, the disclosure does not operate as a waiver in a federal proceeding if the disclosure: (1) would not be a waiver under this rule if it had been made in a federal proceeding; or (2) is not a waiver under the law of the state where the disclosure occurred. (d) Controlling Effect of a Court Order. A federal court may order that the privilege or protection is not waived by disclosure connected wit
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the original intent to fully protect confidential information by allowing the recipient to retain copies 'for recordkeeping purposes.' This contradicts the original text, which required the recipient to 'expunge any Confidential Information' and not keep 'any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof'. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing FRE 502, implicitly supports that parties can contractually agree to specific confidentiality measures. By allowing the retention of copies, the modified text weakens the original agreement and creates a potential conflict.

perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall have no fixed expiration date. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the jurisdiction of any court selected by the vendor.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the expiration date introduces potential ambiguity regarding the agreement's enforceability and violates the premise of contractual law. Changing the jurisdiction clause allows the vendor to select any court. Both of these modifications weaken the Recipient's stance and create issues with contractual law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Statute of Limitations laws (Vary by State)
perturbation.law_citation: Varies by state (e.g., California Code of Civil Procedure § 337)
perturbation.law_explanation: Statutes of limitations set deadlines for filing lawsuits. By removing the expiration date of the confidentiality agreement, it creates uncertainty about when a claim for breach of confidentiality could be brought, potentially conflicting with these laws. California Code of Civil Procedure § 337 sets a statute of limitations for actions based on written contracts; the absence of a defined expiration makes it harder to apply the relevant statute.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2. OF THE TIME OF COMMENCING CIVIL ACTIONS [312 - 366.3] ( Title 2 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 3. The Time of Commencing Actions Other Than for the Recovery of Real Property [335 - 349.4] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 337. Within four years: (a) An action upon any contract, obligation or liability founded upon an instrument in writing, except as provided in Section 336a; provided, that the time within which any action for a money judgment for the balance due upon an obligation for the payment of which a deed of trust or mortgage with power of sale upon real property or any interest therein was given as security, following the exercise of the power of sale in such deed of trust or mortgage, may be brought shall not extend beyond three months after the time of sale under such deed of trust or mortgage. (b) An action to recover (1) upon a book account whether consisting of one or more entries; (2) upon an account stated based upon an account in writing, but the acknowledgment of the account stated need not be in writing; (3) a balance due upon a mutual, open and current account, the items of which are in writing; provided, however, that if an account stated is based upon an
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: CACI No. 360. Nominal Damages :: California Civil Jury Instructions (CACI) (2025) :: Justia CACI No. 360. Nominal Damages Judicial Council of California Civil Jury Instructions (2025 edition) Download PDF 360 . Nominal Damages If you decide that [ name of defendant ] breached the contract but also that [ name of plaintiff ] was not harmed by the breach, you may still award [him/her/ nonbinary pr onoun /it] nominal damages such as one dollar . New September 2003 Sources and Authority • Nominal Damages. Civil Code section 3360. • “A plaintif f is entitled to recover nominal damages for the breach of a contract, despite inability to show that actual damage was inflicted upon him, since the defendant’ s failure to perform a contractual duty is, in itself, a legal wrong that is fully distinct from the actual damages. The maxim that the law will not be concerned with trifles does not, ordinarily , apply to violation of a contractual right. Accordingly , nominal damages, which are presumed as a matter of law to stem merely from the breach of a contract may properly be awarded for the violation of such a right. And, by statute, such is also the rule in California.” ( Sweet v . Johnson (1959) 169 Cal.App.2d 630, 632-633 [337 P .2d 499], internal citations omitted.) • “W ith one exception . . . an unbroken line of cases holds that nominal damages are limited to an amount of a few cents or a dollar .” ( A vina v . Spurlock (1972) 28 Cal.App.3d 1086, 1089 [105 Cal.Rptr . 198], internal citations omitted.) Secondary Sources 1 W itkin, Summary of California Law (1 1th ed. 2017) Contracts, § 903 15 California Forms of Pleading and Practice, Ch. 177, Damages , §§ 177.14, 177.71 (Matthew Bender) 1 Matthew Bender Practice Guide: California Contract Litigation, Ch. 7, Seeking or Opposing Damages in Contract Actions , 7.04[11] 190 © Judicial Council of California . perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the expiration date and allows the vendor to select any court, which introduces potential ambiguity and weakens the recipient's position. The Law Explanation points out that removing the expiration date creates uncertainty about when a claim for breach of confidentiality could be brought, potentially conflicting with statutes of limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 (CCP 337) specifies a four-year statute of limitations for actions upon a written contract. By removing the expiration date, it's unclear when this four-year period begins, creating a direct conflict with the law. The change allows the vendor to select any court, regardless of jurisdiction, undermining the recipient's legal protections.
